title: Neonatal Jaundice
oneLiner: Common, mostly benign, but pathological causes (haemolysis, infection, biliary atresia) can cause kernicterus; treat per NICE thresholds.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Kernicterus (acute bilirubin encephalopathy):** The single finding is *opisthotonus, high-pitched cry, or poor suck* (late signs). Early signs: lethargy, hypotonia. Must check if bilirubin is approaching exchange transfusion threshold on NICE chart.
- **Sepsis (especially late-onset GBS or E. coli):** Jaundice may be the *only* sign. The single finding is *temperature instability or lethargy with poor feeding* in a jaundiced newborn. Perform septic screen (blood culture, CRP, LP) if suspicious.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Yellow discolouration of skin & sclerae (visible day 2-3 in term, day 4-5 in preterm)
  - Poor feeding, sleepiness, lethargy
  - Dark urine (conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia) or pale/chalk stools (biliary atresia)
  - Irritability or high-pitched cry (if encephalopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Assess jaundice extent: cephalocaudal progression (face → trunk → legs)
  - Scleral icterus
  - Assessment of tone, Moro reflex, fontanelle tension
  - Stool colour (ask parent, inspect nappy – green/yellow normal; pale/clay abnormal)
  - Signs of dehydration (dry mouth, sunken eyes, reduced urine output)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Physiological jaundice:** appears >24h, resolves day 10-14; peaks day 3-5. No risk factors, healthy baby.
  - **Breastfeeding jaundice:** exaggerated physiological due to poor feeding/dehydration.
  - **Breast milk jaundice:** persistent after day 7; unconjugated, mild, resolves slowly.
  - **Haemolytic disease (ABO/Rh, G6PD deficiency):** onset <24h, rapid rise, family history, dark urine. Direct Coombs test + if Rh/ABO; G6PD screening if at-risk ethnicity.
  - **Infection (UTI, sepsis, TORCH):** plus temperature instability, poor feeding, CRP ↑, positive culture.
  - **Biliary atresia:** conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (>20% of total), pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly; golden hour for Kasai procedure.
  - **Metabolic (e.g., galactosaemia, hypothyroidism):** conjugated/unconjugated; consider newborn screening results.
- **Splitter:** *Stool colour* – normal straw/yellow = unconjugated/physiological; pale/white/clay = conjugated → must rule out biliary atresia with abdominal ultrasound.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) measurement if gestation ≥35 weeks and baby well – but **confirm with serum if TcB >250 µmol/L or near treatment threshold**.
  - Check stool colour.
- **Bloods:**
  - **Serum bilirubin (total & conjugated/direct)** – plot on NICE chart.
  - **FBC, blood group, direct Coombs test (DAT)**
  - **G6PD screen** if family history, dark urine, or ethnic risk (e.g., African, Mediterranean)
  - **CRP, blood culture** if sepsis suspected
  - **LFTs, TFTs** if conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia
  - **U+E, glucose** if unwell
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal US (if conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia) – look for gallbladder, biliary ducts, choledochal cyst.
  - **Second-line:** HIDA scan if US equivocal.
- **Special tests:**
  - Septic screen (LP, urine culture) if indicated.
  - Urine reducing substances (galactosaemia).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Use **NICE CG98** phototherapy & exchange transfusion thresholds (gestation-specific, risk factor adjusted).
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum bilirubin (total & conjugated) plus DAT** – omission can miss haemolysis requiring IVIG/exchange.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess risk factors: gestation, birth weight, family history, ethnicity, breastfeeding, infection.
  - Plot total bilirubin on NICE treatment threshold graph – **start phototherapy if above threshold**.
  - If approaching exchange transfusion threshold, call paediatric registrar/neonatologist immediately.
  - Ensure adequate hydration – encourage breastfeeding or supplement with formula.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Phototherapy (intensity according to BNF/NICE): blue light (450-470 nm), cover eyes, monitor temperature q1h.
  - Repeat serum bilirubin **4-6 hourly** initially, then less frequent as levels fall.
  - Ensure milk intake – consider IV fluids if vomiting or poor feeding.
  - If isoimmune haemolysis (DAT positive) and bilirubin continues to rise despite phototherapy: discuss IVIG (0.5 g/kg, may repeat) per local policy; prepare for exchange transfusion.
  - If conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: refer to paediatric hepatology, start vitamin K IM (if needed), arrange faecal collection for colour.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once phototherapy stopped: recall if rebound rise (test after 12-24h if risk factors).
  - Conjugated jaundice: follow-up by paediatric GI outpatient clinic for biliary atresia workup.
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Encourage breastfeeding/formula; if phototherapy and dehydration, give **IV 10% dextrose + electrolytes** (e.g. 100 mL/kg/day for term, adjust for weight). Rate [check local policy].
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely indicated (use only if long line + other risk factors; discuss with senior).
  - **Diet:** Continue breast milk or formula; no supplements unless directed.
  - **Observations:** **q1h** during phototherapy (temperature, fluid balance, colour); bilirubin q4-6h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:** **Phototherapy** – no drug dose; intensity defined by local phototherapy unit.
- **IVIG (for isoimmune haemolysis):** **0.5 g/kg IV** over 2-4 hours, may repeat 12h later if no response [BNFc]. Use if bilirubin is rising >8.5 µmol/L/h despite maximal phototherapy.
- **Exchange transfusion:** **Double-volume (85 mL/kg of whole blood)** – performed in NICU; not a drug but critical intervention.
- **Key contraindications:** IVIG – anaphylaxis (rare), IgA deficiency (avoid).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Phototherapy too low – the 'dose' is inadequate light intensity.** Always check light intensity meter and distance from baby. Another: **IVIG infusion too fast** (risk of thrombosis); give over ≥2h.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Kernicterus:** acute bilirubin encephalopathy → seizures, opisthotonus, apnea → permanent brain injury.
  - **Phototherapy side effects:** loose stools, dehydration, hyperthermia, retinal damage if eye covers not used.
  - **Exchange transfusion:** electrolyte disturbances (hypocalcaemia), thrombosis, infection, necrotising enterocolitis.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Athetoid cerebral palsy, hearing loss, dental dysplasia, gaze abnormalities** (if kernicterus).
  - Biliary atresia: cirrhosis, portal hypertension if Kasai delayed.
sections.Prognosis:
- Physiological jaundice: excellent, complete resolution in ≤2 weeks.
- Untreated severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: 30-50% mortality (kernicterus); survivors have lifelong disability.
- Biliary atresia: success of Kasai procedure is time-critical (<60 days) – 50% achieve native liver survival at 2 years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always plot bilirubin on the NICE treatment threshold chart** – do not guess.
- **Check stool colour** in every jaundiced baby – pale stool + conjugated bilirubin = emergency referral.
- **Communicate with midwifery/community** – ensure feeding support and arrange follow-up for bilirubin check after phototherapy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on transcutaneous bilirubin alone** if value is near treatment threshold or baby is <35 weeks.
- **Do not mistake conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia as physiological** – initiate workup for biliary atresia immediately.
- **Do not delay phototherapy** waiting for bilirubin results if clinical judgement suggests rapid rise or risk factors.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Jaundice spreads to legs/palms/soles, baby becomes lethargic or difficult to wake, poor feeding, dark urine or pale stools, fever, or jaundice after day 14.
- **Follow-up:** Community midwife/health visitor within 24-48h; paediatric OPD at 1-2 weeks (or according to local protocol).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call paediatric registrar / neonatologist immediately if:**
  - Bilirubin level approaches exchange transfusion threshold on NICE chart (within 50 µmol/L)
  - Clinical signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (e.g., hypertonia, opisthotonus, high-pitched cry)
  - Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia with pale stools (suspected biliary atresia)
  - Phototherapy fails to lower bilirubin after 6 hours (reduced by <30% or still rising)
  - Baby has sepsis or metabolic crisis
- **ICU / crash team:** If baby has cardiorespiratory instability, seizures, or requires exchange transfusion (usually done in NICU). Use NEWS2 for age-adjusted thresholds – e.g., moderate decompensation score [check local policy].

rawMarkdown:
## Neonatal Jaundice
**One-liner:** Common, mostly benign, but pathological causes (haemolysis, infection, biliary atresia) can cause kernicterus; treat per NICE thresholds.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Kernicterus (acute bilirubin encephalopathy):** The single finding is *opisthotonus, high-pitched cry, or poor suck* (late signs). Early signs: lethargy, hypotonia. Must check if bilirubin is approaching exchange transfusion threshold on NICE chart.
- **Sepsis (especially late-onset GBS or E. coli):** Jaundice may be the *only* sign. The single finding is *temperature instability or lethargy with poor feeding* in a jaundiced newborn. Perform septic screen (blood culture, CRP, LP) if suspicious.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Yellow discolouration of skin & sclerae (visible day 2-3 in term, day 4-5 in preterm)
  - Poor feeding, sleepiness, lethargy
  - Dark urine (conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia) or pale/chalk stools (biliary atresia)
  - Irritability or high-pitched cry (if encephalopathy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Assess jaundice extent: cephalocaudal progression (face → trunk → legs)
  - Scleral icterus
  - Assessment of tone, Moro reflex, fontanelle tension
  - Stool colour (ask parent, inspect nappy – green/yellow normal; pale/clay abnormal)
  - Signs of dehydration (dry mouth, sunken eyes, reduced urine output)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Physiological jaundice:** appears >24h, resolves day 10-14; peaks day 3-5. No risk factors, healthy baby.
  - **Breastfeeding jaundice:** exaggerated physiological due to poor feeding/dehydration.
  - **Breast milk jaundice:** persistent after day 7; unconjugated, mild, resolves slowly.
  - **Haemolytic disease (ABO/Rh, G6PD deficiency):** onset <24h, rapid rise, family history, dark urine. Direct Coombs test + if Rh/ABO; G6PD screening if at-risk ethnicity.
  - **Infection (UTI, sepsis, TORCH):** plus temperature instability, poor feeding, CRP ↑, positive culture.
  - **Biliary atresia:** conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (>20% of total), pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly; golden hour for Kasai procedure.
  - **Metabolic (e.g., galactosaemia, hypothyroidism):** conjugated/unconjugated; consider newborn screening results.
- **Splitter:** *Stool colour* – normal straw/yellow = unconjugated/physiological; pale/white/clay = conjugated → must rule out biliary atresia with abdominal ultrasound.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) measurement if gestation ≥35 weeks and baby well – but **confirm with serum if TcB >250 µmol/L or near treatment threshold**.
  - Check stool colour.
- **Bloods:**
  - **Serum bilirubin (total & conjugated/direct)** – plot on NICE chart.
  - **FBC, blood group, direct Coombs test (DAT)**
  - **G6PD screen** if family history, dark urine, or ethnic risk (e.g., African, Mediterranean)
  - **CRP, blood culture** if sepsis suspected
  - **LFTs, TFTs** if conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia
  - **U+E, glucose** if unwell
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal US (if conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia) – look for gallbladder, biliary ducts, choledochal cyst.
  - **Second-line:** HIDA scan if US equivocal.
- **Special tests:**
  - Septic screen (LP, urine culture) if indicated.
  - Urine reducing substances (galactosaemia).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Use **NICE CG98** phototherapy & exchange transfusion thresholds (gestation-specific, risk factor adjusted).
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum bilirubin (total & conjugated) plus DAT** – omission can miss haemolysis requiring IVIG/exchange.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess risk factors: gestation, birth weight, family history, ethnicity, breastfeeding, infection.
  - Plot total bilirubin on NICE treatment threshold graph – **start phototherapy if above threshold**.
  - If approaching exchange transfusion threshold, call paediatric registrar/neonatologist immediately.
  - Ensure adequate hydration – encourage breastfeeding or supplement with formula.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Phototherapy (intensity according to BNF/NICE): blue light (450-470 nm), cover eyes, monitor temperature q1h.
  - Repeat serum bilirubin **4-6 hourly** initially, then less frequent as levels fall.
  - Ensure milk intake – consider IV fluids if vomiting or poor feeding.
  - If isoimmune haemolysis (DAT positive) and bilirubin continues to rise despite phototherapy: discuss IVIG (0.5 g/kg, may repeat) per local policy; prepare for exchange transfusion.
  - If conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: refer to paediatric hepatology, start vitamin K IM (if needed), arrange faecal collection for colour.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once phototherapy stopped: recall if rebound rise (test after 12-24h if risk factors).
  - Conjugated jaundice: follow-up by paediatric GI outpatient clinic for biliary atresia workup.
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Encourage breastfeeding/formula; if phototherapy and dehydration, give **IV 10% dextrose + electrolytes** (e.g. 100 mL/kg/day for term, adjust for weight). Rate [check local policy].
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely indicated (use only if long line + other risk factors; discuss with senior).
  - **Diet:** Continue breast milk or formula; no supplements unless directed.
  - **Observations:** **q1h** during phototherapy (temperature, fluid balance, colour); bilirubin q4-6h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:** **Phototherapy** – no drug dose; intensity defined by local phototherapy unit.
- **IVIG (for isoimmune haemolysis):** **0.5 g/kg IV** over 2-4 hours, may repeat 12h later if no response [BNFc]. Use if bilirubin is rising >8.5 µmol/L/h despite maximal phototherapy.
- **Exchange transfusion:** **Double-volume (85 mL/kg of whole blood)** – performed in NICU; not a drug but critical intervention.
- **Key contraindications:** IVIG – anaphylaxis (rare), IgA deficiency (avoid).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Phototherapy too low – the 'dose' is inadequate light intensity.** Always check light intensity meter and distance from baby. Another: **IVIG infusion too fast** (risk of thrombosis); give over ≥2h.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Kernicterus:** acute bilirubin encephalopathy → seizures, opisthotonus, apnea → permanent brain injury.
  - **Phototherapy side effects:** loose stools, dehydration, hyperthermia, retinal damage if eye covers not used.
  - **Exchange transfusion:** electrolyte disturbances (hypocalcaemia), thrombosis, infection, necrotising enterocolitis.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Athetoid cerebral palsy, hearing loss, dental dysplasia, gaze abnormalities** (if kernicterus).
  - Biliary atresia: cirrhosis, portal hypertension if Kasai delayed.

### Prognosis
- Physiological jaundice: excellent, complete resolution in ≤2 weeks.
- Untreated severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: 30-50% mortality (kernicterus); survivors have lifelong disability.
- Biliary atresia: success of Kasai procedure is time-critical (<60 days) – 50% achieve native liver survival at 2 years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always plot bilirubin on the NICE treatment threshold chart** – do not guess.
- **Check stool colour** in every jaundiced baby – pale stool + conjugated bilirubin = emergency referral.
- **Communicate with midwifery/community** – ensure feeding support and arrange follow-up for bilirubin check after phototherapy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on transcutaneous bilirubin alone** if value is near treatment threshold or baby is <35 weeks.
- **Do not mistake conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia as physiological** – initiate workup for biliary atresia immediately.
- **Do not delay phototherapy** waiting for bilirubin results if clinical judgement suggests rapid rise or risk factors.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Jaundice spreads to legs/palms/soles, baby becomes lethargic or difficult to wake, poor feeding, dark urine or pale stools, fever, or jaundice after day 14.
- **Follow-up:** Community midwife/health visitor within 24-48h; paediatric OPD at 1-2 weeks (or according to local protocol).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call paediatric registrar / neonatologist immediately if:**
  - Bilirubin level approaches exchange transfusion threshold on NICE chart (within 50 µmol/L)
  - Clinical signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (e.g., hypertonia, opisthotonus, high-pitched cry)
  - Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia with pale stools (suspected biliary atresia)
  - Phototherapy fails to lower bilirubin after 6 hours (reduced by <30% or still rising)
  - Baby has sepsis or metabolic crisis
- **ICU / crash team:** If baby has cardiorespiratory instability, seizures, or requires exchange transfusion (usually done in NICU). Use NEWS2 for age-adjusted thresholds – e.g., moderate decompensation score [check local policy].
